evals.name: fapi-2.0-explainer
evals.prompt: I need to walk our security architecture review board through FAPI 2.0 next Tuesday — about half the room is execs who've never heard of it and half are practitioners who think FAPI 1 Advanced is still current. Build me an educational site (single HTML file) explaining FAPI 2.0: what it is, how it differs from FAPI 1.0 Advanced, where it sits in the OAuth/OIDC stack, and why a Tier 1 bank should care. Save it as fapi-2.0-explained.html in the current directory.
evals.expected_output: Single self-contained HTML file with TL;DR, audience switcher, Mermaid flow diagram, FAPI 1 vs 2 comparison table, regulatory callouts, glossary, citations.

assertions.text: A single .html file named fapi-2.0-explained.html exists in the outputs directory
HTML inlines all CSS in a <style> block; no <link rel=stylesheet> to external CSS (Mermaid CDN is the only allowed external resource)
Page contains an up-front TL;DR or executive summary section visible to all audiences
Page contains a working exec/practitioner audience switcher (buttons + practitioner-only class + JS toggle)
Page embeds at least one Mermaid diagram (class="mermaid" or <pre class="mermaid">)
Page contains a <table> comparing FAPI 1.0 Advanced and FAPI 2.0
Page contains a glossary section (Glossary heading or <dl> with key terms)
Page contains a Further Reading or References section with external links to primary sources (openid.net, FAPI working group, etc.)
No emoji characters appear in rendered body text
Page references OAuth 2.0 and/or OpenID Connect as the underlying stack
Page mentions DPoP or mTLS sender-constrained tokens (a defining FAPI 2.0 feature)
Page contains at least one regulatory callout referencing Open Banking, MAS, APRA, PSD2/PSD3, or another applicable regime — explaining why a Tier 1 bank should care

evals.name: pci-dss-4.0-scope-reduction
evals.prompt: Build me a one-pager (single self-contained HTML file) on PCI-DSS 4.0 scope reduction strategies. Audience is mixed — our CISO will read it, but so will the platform engineers actually implementing tokenization and segmentation. Cover: what counts as in-scope under 4.0, the main scope-reduction levers (tokenization, network segmentation, scoped CDE patterns), and what changed in v4.0 vs v3.2.1 around scope. Make sure to cite the actual requirement numbers. Save as pci-dss-4.0-scope-reduction.html.
evals.expected_output: Single self-contained HTML file with TL;DR, audience switcher, CDE-boundary Mermaid diagram, v3.2.1 vs v4.0 table, regulatory callouts citing specific PCI-DSS requirement numbers, glossary, citations.

assertions.text: A single .html file named pci-dss-4.0-scope-reduction.html exists in the outputs directory
HTML inlines all CSS in a <style> block; no <link rel=stylesheet> to external CSS
Page contains an up-front TL;DR or executive summary section
Page contains a working exec/practitioner audience switcher
Page embeds at least one Mermaid diagram
Page contains a <table> comparing v3.2.1 and v4.0 on scope
Page contains a glossary section defining CDE and tokenization at minimum
Page contains a Further Reading section linking to PCI SSC primary sources (pcisecuritystandards.org)
No emoji characters in rendered body text
Page contains at least three specific PCI-DSS requirement number references (e.g., 'Req 1.4', 'Requirement 3.5', '8.3.6')
Page covers both tokenization and network segmentation as scope-reduction levers
Page references the cardholder data environment (CDE) by name

evals.name: spiffe-spire-workload-identity
evals.prompt: Create an educational site explaining SPIFFE and SPIRE for workload identity — single HTML file, professional enterprise styling. The reader is a platform engineer onboarding to our zero-trust east-west program, but their manager (a director, not super technical) will also read it. Cover: the SPIFFE identity model, what a SPIFFE ID looks like, what SPIRE does as a reference implementation, how SVIDs (x.509 and JWT) work, and how this fits into a service mesh + mTLS story. Show the identity provenance on the diagrams. Save as spiffe-spire-workload-identity.html.
evals.expected_output: Single self-contained HTML file with TL;DR, audience switcher, Mermaid mTLS-with-SPIFFE-ID diagram, x.509 vs JWT SVID table, code snippet showing SPIFFE ID format or SPIRE registration entry, glossary, citations.

assertions.text: A single .html file named spiffe-spire-workload-identity.html exists in the outputs directory
HTML inlines all CSS in a <style> block; no <link rel=stylesheet> to external CSS
Page contains an up-front TL;DR or executive summary section
Page contains a working exec/practitioner audience switcher
Page embeds at least one Mermaid diagram
Page contains a <table> comparing x.509 SVID and JWT SVID
Page contains a glossary section defining SPIFFE, SPIRE, SVID, and Workload API
Page contains a Further Reading section linking to spiffe.io or CNCF primary sources
No emoji characters in rendered body text
Page shows at least one example SPIFFE ID in spiffe://trust-domain/workload format (in code or prose)
Page explains both x.509 SVID and JWT SVID
Page contains at least one <pre><code> block (e.g., SPIRE registration entry, SPIFFE ID example, kubectl/spire-server snippet)
Mermaid diagram labels east-west calls with SPIFFE IDs or trust-domain identifiers
